   • A small tail of capital‐efficient or asset‐heavy businesses (e.g., manufacturing, restaurants) may have higher headcounts.
4. Possible data sources:
   • Crunchbase/PitchBook headcount fields.  
   • NVCA or university surveys of early‐stage companies.  
   • Bureau of Labor Statistics small‐business employment surveys.
5. Influential factors:
   • Industry labor‐intensity (retail vs. consulting vs. manufacturing).  
   • Stage of maturity at time of funding.  
   • Geographic labor costs.  
   • Founders’ hiring strategy.
6. Potential biases/limitations:
   • Reporting bias – small firms underreport headcount.  
   • Survivorship bias – only companies still active in databases.  
   • Definition differences (full‐time equivalents vs. contractors).
7. Assumptions:
   • “Average” refers to the long‐run mean across this population.  
   • No systematic shift by region or time beyond typical variation.  
   • Data sources reasonably represent the population.
8. Temporal/subgroup variation:

   • Certain sub‐sectors (e.g., healthcare services) hire more staff for the same funding.
9. Quantile estimates (population mean):

   • 50th percentile ≈ 25 employees – typical early‐stage non‐tech staffed at two dozen.  
   • 75th percentile ≈ 50 employees – more labor‐intensive or asset‐heavy.  
   • 95th percentile ≈ 120 employees – rare cases (e.g., small‐scale manufacturing, restaurant chains).
10. Context: Seed‐ or series A‐type rounds; non‐software firms often require onsite staff or manual processes, boosting headcounts.

12. Focus on population‐mean uncertainty: These quantiles reflect our uncertainty about the true average headcount, not spreads across individual firms.
13. Spread interpretation: Moderate – we’re fairly sure most averages lie below 50 but allow for a small chance of much larger means.
14. Edge cases: Some ultra‐efficient digital or consulting shops may stay under 5 employees; some franchise‐type ventures approach 200 employees on small capital.

   - Sector subcategory (retail vs. manufacturing vs. services).

6. Potential biases/limitations:
   - “Non‐technology” classification is broad and inconsistently applied.
   - Publicly reported headcounts may lag real‐time changes.
   - Sample bias toward companies that disclose more data.

7. Assumptions:
   - “Non‐technology” excludes biotech, medtech (treated as tech).
   - We focus on current typical headcount at the time they had raised \$6–20 M.
   - No major structural shift in staffing norms in the last 2–3 years.

8. Time/subgroup effects:
   - Remote/hybrid trends could slightly lower headcounts for service startups.
   - Economic cycles (e.g., post-2020 hiring booms) may push means upward over time.

9. Quantile estimates (reflecting uncertainty about the population mean):
   - 5th percentile ≈ 20 employees: only a small chance the true mean is below this (very lean operations).
   - 25th percentile ≈ 35 employees: many small non-tech startups will be around tens of employees.
   - 50th percentile (median) ≈ 60 employees: central estimate from anecdotal and published data.
   - 75th percentile ≈ 110 employees: accounts for labor-heavy business models.
   - 95th percentile ≈ 250 employees: rare, high-staffed companies (retail chains, hospitality groups).

10. Contextual facts:
   - Typical Series A tech startups have ~50 employees; non-tech may trend slightly higher but not dramatically so.
   - Companies raising \$6–20 M are usually beyond seed and have established teams.

11. Justification of choices:
   - Lognormal ensures positivity and accommodates a long right tail.
   - Quantiles drawn from industry benchmarks and expert reports.

12. Emphasis on population‐level uncertainty:
   - These quantiles describe my uncertainty about the true average headcount across all such companies, not the variation within one company.

13. Spread reflection:
   - The wide spread (20 to 250) indicates moderate uncertainty: likely a few dozen to low hundreds, but tail risk exists.

14. Edge cases/alternative scenarios:
   - A consumer retail chain might have an average headcount >300, but this is very unlikely.
   - Very lean service startups (consultancies) might keep headcount below 20, but that’s rare once they’ve raised \$6 M+.
